words: LAURA SCHRAGER: Where would you sell them?
MAY LeMARR: There would be other people who would come in from outside to pick
huckleberries also. And they would see us with our berries and they
liked them awfully well so they'd buy what we picked that day.

speaker: LJ
words: We didn't even get home with them.

speaker: ML
words: And borne with empty pails, which was fine by us.

speaker: LJ
words: Couple dollars in our pocket.

speaker: ML
words: That meant a lot. There again, it helped provide the school clothes and

speaker: ML
words: books and things that we needed for school in days.

speaker: LJ
words: A dollar a gallon we got for them then during WWI...

speaker: ML
words: We got dollars a gallon during WWI.

speaker: LJ
words: Two dollars, was it? Anyway, we thought it was a lot.

speaker: ML
words: It was quite a bit in those days.But ordinarily they would sell for a

speaker: ML
words: dollar a gallon so everything else went up in price.

speaker: LJ
words: And we're still selling huckleberries. We're getting five dollars a gallon

speaker: LJ
words: now. We're getting rich.(laughs)

speaker: LS
words: Doesn't buy what it bought back then.

speaker: LJ
words: Doesn't buy much now. Tank of gas. Don't even do that.The little car

speaker: LJ
words: even takes more than that. The big one takes about ten.

speaker: LS
words: With the two of you, did you do a lot of things together?

speaker: ML
words: Quite a bit. I was about eleven then. In 1918 I was ll^in 1917. And 1918

speaker: ML
words: we picked a lot of berries. I was eleven years old. I was a pretty good

speaker: ML
words: sized girl. I could pick my share, I guess.

speaker: LJ
words: She still can. She's a better picker than I am andl been picking for 65

speaker: LJ
words: years. May's been picking for about 60, I guess.

speaker: ML
words: I sold my first huckleberries when I was nine. Sold them to a man, we three

speaker: ML
words: kids were out picking. No, the four of us. The two boys and Molly and I.

speaker: ML
words: And so at the end of that time we had our pails full.I had a half a gallon

speaker: ML
words: lard pail. The other kids had their gallon buckets. So this fellow said

speaker: ML
words: "I'll just buy your huckleberries from you." Give the other kids each

speaker: ML
words: a dollar. So here he gave me a dollar for my half a gallon. And Alfred, the

speaker: ML
words: one that was here, said,"Oh my goodness, that's too much, she only has a

speaker: ML
words: half a gallon."Well,"She said she worked just as hard as the rest of you

speaker: ML
words: did."So I had my dollar for my half a gallon.I was pretty proud about that.

speaker: ML
words: Big silver dollar in those days.No paper money. That was a big help in

speaker: ML
words: bringing home extra money. Money was so hard to get a hold of in those

speaker: ML
words: days.

speaker: LS
words: Would you go out often to pick berries?

speaker: ML
words: Oh yes, we all during the season.The huckleberries would ripen

speaker: ML
words: after the fourth, usually, the Fourth of July. And we could pick then up

speaker: ML
words: until about the first of August. And people used to come out to the house,

speaker: ML
words: local people from Bovill come out there, they knew we had huckleberries,

speaker: ML
words: they'd come back there and buy them. From us. Or sometimes we'd take

speaker: ML
words: them into a grocery and trade them for groceries. So in a lot of ways

speaker: ML
words: that worked to our advantage. Give us a chance to pick up a little extra

speaker: ML
words: money.

speaker: LS
words: Would you go out everyday?

speaker: ML
words: Usually. As a rule we'd go every day. We'd make a regular job of it.Except

speaker: ML
words: during haying we mighty lay off and help shock hay that we did out in the

speaker: ML
words: field whey the hay would be mowed down, we'd have to put it in shocks so

speaker: ML
words: they could go along and pick it up on a wagon and it it to the barn.

speaker: ML
words: So we had to help on thet in between times. But as a rule, we'd stay with

speaker: ML
words: our berry picking pretty regular. Although not every year we'd have berries.

speaker: ML
words: That's another thing. Because every other year, the berry crop would be

speaker: ML
words: good.

speaker: LS
words: You figured every otker?

speaker: ML
words: Usually. Every other year that they would be good. There would always be

speaker: ML
words: some somewhere but, nearby, but they don't produce equally good each year.

speaker: ML
words: But we enjoyed it. It was kind of hard work, but we always it

speaker: ML
words: Being out in the woods, and making a little extra money.

speaker: LS
words: I heard that the Indians would shake the berry bush.

speaker: ML
words: Yes.They used to cut the -bushes and^I can't understand, because

speaker: ML
words: you would think that they'd want to p%serve things like that more. Iknow

speaker: ML
words: it would taKe some time for that bush to grow back. But I've heard of it,

speaker: ML
words: I never did see it, but I've heard of hugh piles of broken huckleberry

speaker: ML
words: bushes. And they'd beat them off onto a blanket. And I guess the blanket

speaker: ML
words: might be woolen or something like that. And then they could shake it a

speaker: ML
words: little bit like that and the leaves,Adebris would stick to the blanket.

speaker: ML
words: The berries would role off. And they would also sell their berries, I think.

speaker: ML
words: To a large extent. I've heard of them doing thatr But I never did see

speaker: ML
words: them do that, but I think my sister has. When she comes down I think she

speaker: ML
words: can tell us somewhat about that too. I never liked that idea. I'd rather

speaker: ML
words: just go and pick 'em. I wouldn't see much fun in working in a bunch of

speaker: ML
words: broken bushes.

speaker: LS
words: Were you able to go to school?

speaker: ML
words: Not very much. In fact,I didn't start school until I was almost eleven.

speaker: ML
words: But I could read and write fairly well before I attended school. Learned

speaker: ML
words: that at home. Well _ had a first grade reader, I guess, at home. And I

speaker: ML
words: got a little start. I think my brother got me started on that or mother did.

speaker: ML
words: One or the other. But I just naturally liked to read, so IApicked it up.

speaker: ML
words: I didn't get school in until I was almost eleven, but I was promoted to

speaker: ML
words: the third grade at the end of that school term.And being that I could

speaker: ML
words: read and I enjoyed it, I was promoted, so I ggt through grade school in

speaker: ML
words: a hurry that way. I always made two grades a year. Or more. So it wasn't

speaker: ML
words: a problem that way. I can't imagine a life without reading. One of the

speaker: ML
words: things I still enjoy doing.

speaker: LS
words: What were you able to, what kinds of books were around then?

speaker: ML
words: Well I can remember, I wish I had kept them, but i didn't. But they were,

speaker: ML
words: well, Alice in Wonderland and The Fisherman's Wife. Can I even remember

speaker: ML
words: back to all the things we had. But we read those stories after that fashion.

speaker: ML
words: And Three Mttle Pigs that would be in the second grade reader, I guess.

speaker: ML
words: I don't know if they'd be called McGuffy's Reader or not. I suppose

speaker: ML
words: would. I think it maybe would be something of that order. But that would

speaker: ML
words: be the type of stories we had to read. Black Beauty And then as we got

speaker: ML
words: a little further a long the grades, there'd be all the poems by Longfellow

speaker: ML
words: and all those that we'd have to memorize and things like that. That'd

speaker: ML
words: six and seventh grade that we'd have that.

speaker: LS
words: Were they still having literaries when you were young?

speaker: ML
words: Yes, I think they did. But they used to have that in Helmer. Literary cttfedL

speaker: ML
words: meetings, but I never attended. I was too small them. To young gor that.

speaker: ML
words: Now, my brother Alfred would know about that.He's attended some. But I

speaker: ML
words: never did go to one of those. But then, too, that quite a ways

speaker: ML
words: away. That would usually be held at night, and be about four miles away.

speaker: ML
words: And usually in the winter. there'd be four, five foot of snow on the

speaker: ML
words: ground. So due to that, a person was more or less isolated in the winter.

speaker: ML
words: It would be hard to get out very much.

speaker: LS
words: Would you stay at home?

speaker: ML
words: Uh huh. That's about all we did. Stay home. The boys stayed busy chopping

speaker: ML
words: wood and everything. Course, they .used to sell wood. Chop down trees and

speaker: ML
words: sell wood by the cord in Bovill. Haul that out. And then to go out for

speaker: ML
words: the mail once a week, while it was at Helmer. Then we moved to Bovill,

speaker: ML
words: where the mail came to. So they would have to go there to get the mail and

speaker: ML
words: we had weekly newspapers in those days. Mother would get her Norwegian

speaker: ML
words: newspaper, tt came from Decora,Iowa. That was aweekly paper. And the

speaker: ML
words: Spokesman-Review. That was a weekly paper. Finally I think they got it

speaker: ML
words: up to where it was twice aweek. And it was just mailed out to subscribers.

speaker: ML
words: And had amagazine called the Idaho Farmer. That was published here

speaker: ML
words: in Spokane Ithink. Near as Ican remember. And that would be, seemed like

speaker: ML
words: that came once a month. I would have to ask my sister. And that had stories

speaker: ML
words: in it.Continued stories. And we'd all look forward to that because it would

speaker: ML
words: be something interesting to read. Ask her about the Idaho Farmer.

speaker: LS
words: Would you read those out load to each other?

speaker: ML
words: No, usually we'd read it to ourselves, although mother spoke both English

speaker: ML
words: and Norwegian, but Norwegain she spoke fluently, and English not to, clearly.

speaker: ML
words: But mother would read from her paper. There was lots of stories in her

speaker: ML
words: Norwegian paper that she would read out load to us in Norwegian, which we

speaker: ML
words: understood very well.So that was another diversion.

speaker: LS
words: Was your father Norwegian?

speaker: ML
words: Yes. And...

speaker: LS
words: Did you learn Norwegian at home?

speaker: ML
words: It was a strange setup we had on that. I'll never explain how, but mother

speaker: ML
words: always talked Norwegian to us at home. I just grew up on that. But we

speaker: ML
words: answered in English. Now, I don't know why. Seems strange. I should be

speaker: ML
words: able to speak it fluently. But Idon't. I can get by, make myself understood.

speaker: ML
words: But mom would say something in Norwegian to us and we always answer m

speaker: ML
words: English, invariably. Dad spoke Norwegian -of course, as well as

speaker: ML
words: English. He spoke that very well. So it was mother that was alittle bit

speaker: ML
words: more halting with hers. But she could read and write both.Dad I think

speaker: ML
words: only read Norwegain. Idon't think he read English too well. But Ican't

speaker: ML
words: understand how that happened.

speaker: LS
words: How did you pick up your English?

speaker: ML
words: I don't know. Must have been from dad. I don't really know. 'Cause as

speaker: ML
words: far back as I can remember, she always talked Norwegian to us and we answered

speaker: ML
words: in English. But we didn't have any close neighbors, you know. That had

speaker: ML
words: children our own age. I guess my sister did more, but then when I came

speaker: ML
words: along, I was more than six years younger than my sister, there weren't

speaker: ML
words: any kids around my age then. All the other people had moved away. That had

speaker: ML
words: any youngsters.

speaker: LS
words: Your sister mentioned that you'd seen the Indians pick berries.

speaker: LS
words: You've seen 'em. And break bushes.

speaker: LJ
words: Break bushes, I think the squaws, what they call 'em, the sqaws. They sit

speaker: LJ
words: down and picked the berries and the men, or was it vice versa?.

speaker: LJ
words: They'd probably go out and break the bushes.

speaker: LJ
words: And the- squaws usually have to do all the work anyway, so I imagine they

speaker: LJ
words: had to break the bushes and bring it to the...

speaker: LJ
words: Yeah, it could be. I never did see them do it, but I've seen the bushes.

speaker: LJ
words: They'd be piled high as this room.

speaker: LJ
words: You saw them do it then actually?

speaker: LJ
words: Oh yeah. I never did 'cause that was a little bit before my time, 'cause the Indians

speaker: LJ
words: didn t come around, well not quite as much, it began to diminish, didn't

speaker: LJ
words: it, as time went on. I remember them coming there and getting water out of

speaker: LJ
words: this well I was telling you about, that was such good spring water coming

speaker: LJ
words: up on the horse and they'd fill their little canvas sacks with water.

speaker: LJ
words: Drinking water.

speaker: LJ
words: And dad had to get after them because they were digging camas.In the you know,

speaker: LJ
words: and it made it so rough when they mowed the hay. And I

speaker: LJ
words: guess one of them was threatening, wasn't he?

speaker: ML
words: Oh did he?

speaker: LJ
words: Yeah, I think that's what dad said. 'Cause he chased him out of the meadow.

speaker: LJ
words: That's what they made their bread.(pause)

speaker: LJ
words: Half a mile or something like that and my dad came after me, we

speaker: LJ
words: were just eating dinner. I can remember, I must have been awful naughty.

speaker: LJ
words: I was kicking the table. He was grabbing me and I was kicking away. I didn't

speaker: LJ
words: want to go home. I wanted to but I got it afterwards I got home

speaker: LJ
words: I got a spanking. I remember that. That's before May, I guess I must have

speaker: LJ
words: been about four years old. But that's about i remember, any unusual things.

speaker: LS
words: Were there, did you ever get things like ice cream and candy?

speaker: LJ
words: Well my dad, whenever he'd go to town, they'd always buy a nickle's worth

speaker: LJ
words: of candy and he got a big sack of candy for a nickle, you know. He'd come

speaker: LJ
words: home with candy or maybe some oranges or bananas or something like that.

speaker: LJ
words: If he could, all depends how they could afford it. If they had any money

speaker: LJ
words: left.And then he used to go to Deary and done most of the trading and that

speaker: LJ
words: was about eight miles. And of course, whenever he paid up his bills, why

speaker: LJ
words: they'd give him quite a sack of candy. Maybe about a pound or a pound and

speaker: LJ
words: a half, something like that.And of course, we was all hungry for sweets,

speaker: LJ
words: that didn't last too long.And when my brother went to school down at Helmer,

speaker: LJ
words: we used to have a Christmas program down there, I was too small to go at

speaker: LJ
words: that time, but anyway we'd always, I think my dad donated maybe two bits or

speaker: LJ
words: something like that or a sack of candy or an orange or apples, or something

speaker: LJ
words: like that we'd get. And we'd come horn that and. or peanuts. And that

speaker: LJ
words: would be our treat for Christmas.And maybe we'd have a little tree or

speaker: LJ
words: something and we'd have to make our own decoration out of catalog , you

speaker: LJ
words: know, and it'd be colored. And we'd paste them together and string 'em around

speaker: LJ
words: on the tree. Did you ever do that when you was a kid?

speaker: LS
words: Not from the catalog.

speaker: LJ
words: That had the pretty pictures on it. And popcorn, sometimes we'd string

speaker: LJ
words: popcorn and stuff like that for Christmas. Make it look Christmas.

speaker: LJ
words: But as far as the eats go, we didn't have turkey or anything like that.

speaker: LS
words: Did your parents keep any of the Norwegian customs?

speaker: LJ
words: Our mother did have, but we moved so much it seemed like, the only thing

speaker: LJ
words: I got which I think would be nice if you'd want it for the museum is her

speaker: LJ
words: wedding veil. It's kind of tattered. And I have their wedding picture.

speaker: LS
words: I was asking if they kept traditions from Norway.

speaker: LJ
words: Mother wasn't born in Norway. She was born in the United States. She was

speaker: LJ
words: born in Illinois. But her mother and dad and her sisters, her oldest sister,

speaker: LJ
words: mother was the baby of the family. And they were from Norway. And if I

speaker: LJ
words: remember right, I guess the brother next to her must have been, had died

speaker: LJ
words: when they were on the ship and he was buried at sea.'Cause they couldn't

speaker: LJ
words: take the body on, you know. But they went to Illinois, Filmore County, illinois I

speaker: LJ
words: guess is where it was,AThey moved to Minnesota. She was about, what

speaker: LJ
words: did she say she was? Baby I guess. Maybe about a year old or something like

speaker: LJ
words: that.

speaker: LS
words: Did your parents speak Norwegian?

speaker: LJ
words: Yes, my mother spoke Norwegian all the time. But she could speak English

speaker: LJ
words: very good and she wrote Norwegian and English both. And read the papers

speaker: LJ
words: her and all that and the Bible. Of course, that was main thing. And wed

speaker: LJ
words: have to be awfully quiet when she read the Bible, We'd have to pay

speaker: LJ
words: attention.

speaker: LS
words: Would she read it in Norwegian?

speaker: LJ
words: Yeah, she'd read it in Norwegian. Yeah, we could speak a little Norwegian.

speaker: LJ
words: May and I went to Norway five years ago. Had a wonderful time there. Trying

speaker: LJ
words: to find some of our relatives there, but...(pause)Dad, he always talked

speaker: LJ
words: English to us girls, kids, rather. But mother'd always talk Norwegian. 'Cause

speaker: LJ
words: she wanted us to know the Norwegian language. But dad, and then when they

speaker: LJ
words: talked, why they used the Norwegian language together. But we could under

speaker: LJ
words: stand. I was talking to my brother here not too long ago and I'd say

speaker: LJ
words: something in Norwegian and you know, by golly, he's forgotten, he hasn't

speaker: LJ
words: been around it since mother died. That's been several years ago. '45,'46

speaker: LJ
words: But when you're around it all comes back to you.

speaker: LJ
words: Yeah, it's quite a thing. I wished I'd learned to read it and write it.

speaker: LJ
words: Course there wasn't any Norwegian schools around there. Now like here in

speaker: LJ
words: Spokane,there's Sons of Norway, they have schools(pause) And she was a

speaker: LJ
words: midwife, I think. But they came from the south. Boy, was she ever a good

speaker: LJ
words: cook. Wonderful cook.AI remember the first time I ate there,you know you

speaker: LJ
words: kind of feel that it wasn't clean but it was clean as could be.Very much so.

speaker: LS
words: Were the Wells' well accepted?

speaker: LJ
words: In Deary? Yes they were. It's funny, one time old Joe Wells said,"Well you

speaker: LJ
words: know, I'm the only white man here. All the rest of them are Swedes."(laughs)

speaker: LJ
words: And he was nice people. They accepted them anyway. Later on their daughter got married

speaker: LJ
words: and they had a couple of children I think. And the daughter was a very

speaker: LJ
words: smart girl. She was valedictorian, But we know they didn't, something

speaker: LJ
words: about it because she was black, they didn't want to give it to her. But

speaker: LJ
words: I guess the rest of them kicked about it so she got it. I thought that

speaker: LJ
words: was kind of mean.

speaker: LS
words: Was she much older than you?

speaker: LJ
words: Oh yeah. The granddaughter wouldn't have been older than I am 'cause I

speaker: LJ
words: can remember when she was born, 'cause I went up to see her. I wanted to

speaker: LJ
words: see what the baby looked like. (Pause) That was the main thing. Get out

speaker: LJ
words: and earn some money. That was during pretty hard times too. What year

speaker: LJ
words: we down there?

speaker: ML
words: Let me think. 1923 we went down to Princeton to work.

speaker: LJ
words: '23, uh huh, that's right.

speaker: ML
words: 'Cause you got married the next year in 1924.

speaker: LJ
words: That's right.

speaker: LS
words: You'd worked by Shea Meadows?

speaker: LJ
words: Yeah,...

speaker: ML
words: That was in 1922 then, Summer of 1922. Both gone up to get some school. And he

speaker: ML
words: was black as the ace of spades. She was too. But they were pretty

speaker: ML
words: money.

speaker: LJ
words: That's '22. Then we went down to Camp One. And then from there to Camp 7,

speaker: LJ
words: Back to Shea Meadows again.(laughs) How long did you work there? I can't

speaker: LJ
words: remember?

speaker: ML
words: Camp Seven? Why see I started in Princeton and in December we were transfered

speaker: ML
words: to Camp £even. And I worked there til the following August I think. And I

speaker: ML
words: quite and I went back to finish grade school.(laughs)

speaker: LJ
words: That's right.

speaker: ML
words: Went back to school and finished that. I went back to work, I guess the

speaker: ML
words: next year I went back to the logging camp. I worked on and off for about

speaker: ML
words: seven and a half or eight years. Potlatch Lumber Company.

speaker: LJ
words: Y'ou worked for Billy Musch. didn't you?

speaker: ML
words: Up to headquarters. That was in 1928. I was there for a while.

speaker: LS
words: Were they just starting logging that area? When you went out there?

speaker: ML
words: No, the camps around Elk River had been running for a number of years.

speaker: ML
words: But they had built better camps and enlarged a lot and they called Camp

speaker: ML
words: C headquarters where it was high up where the logging engines would come

speaker: ML
words: in for the night. They kept them there. That was the base for them. And

speaker: ML
words: then they'd go to the other two or three camps and pick uplogs during the

speaker: ML
words: day. But the train crew always tied up there at camp C.

speaker: LS
words: That was near Elk River?

speaker: ML
words: About fourteen miles from Elk River. The way the crow flies, I guess about fourteen,

speaker: ML
words: fifteen.

speaker: LS
words: Were you out there then?

speaker: LJ
words: No, we were down at Camp 5 I guess. On Hog Meadows.

speaker: ML
words: About that time. Then you were transferred up to Camp 8 at Elk River also.

speaker: LJ
words: Yeah, I stayed down at^Hog Meadows all alone there, Betty and I. And I'd

speaker: LJ
words: bar myself into the shack andNknives up so nobody would get in. I was kind

speaker: LJ
words: of worried being by ourself. And then in the morning I'd go down aid get

speaker: LJ
words: then in the morning I'd go down and get breakfast in the logging camp, they

speaker: LJ
words: had 3 whole camp there. I had to go clear down and start the pump at the

speaker: LJ
words: pumphouse. Started that at night.See if it run alright and then build

speaker: LJ
words: a fire down there, kept the fire going so we wouldn't freeze up in the

speaker: LJ
words: wintertime. Then Betty and I'd go back to our shack and bar ourselves in.

speaker: LJ
words: (laughs) I don't know what good it done. If anybody wanted to get in. Then

speaker: LJ
words: from there, they got aplace for us up at Camp Dwhere Albert was cooking.

speaker: LJ
words: They took him up there. So we was there, then you came up there later, didn't

speaker: LJ
words: you??

speaker: LJ
words: Yes, I came up later then.

speaker: LJ
words: That's where she met her husband. First husband.

speaker: LJ
words: Camp D. I was there til 1930. Then, about two years up there.

speaker: LJ
words: When you worked together, were jobs any different from each others?

speaker: LJ
words: No, when we worked together we done the same thing. I mean, wait table,

speaker: LJ
words: that's down in Princeton. Over at Camp Seven also.

speaker: ML
words: They served family style at^logging camps. Our job was just keep the

speaker: ML
words: dishes filled.Vegetables and meats.

speaker: LS
words: why did you have to get up at four thirty?

speaker: LJ
words: You had a lot to do. You had to get your table, course, we had a lot of

speaker: LJ
words: silverware and plates on and cups and all that.

speaker: ML
words: You put those on the night before. But there would be butter to put on the

speaker: ML
words: tables and milk. Fruit and things for their breakfast.

speaker: LJ
words: And all their cookies and doughnuts.

speaker: ML
words: We had to have bread on the table. BeAslice of bread, there'd always be

speaker: ML
words: somebody who'd want it. In addition to that, then they'd have pancakes every

speaker: ML
words: morning. Toast. Hash brown potatoes. Bacon, eggs.

speaker: ML
words: Would they have eggs every morning?

speaker: ML
words: Every morning. Scrambled and boiled and fried.

speaker: ML
words: They'd have hot cereal. And we'd have to put milk on for their cereal, so

speaker: ML
words: we d have to canned milk is the way we did it. As well as a can of

speaker: ML
words: milk for their coffee, for their coffee cream and you'd dilute a certain

speaker: ML
words: amount of that milk for their cereal. Then we always had dry cereal and

speaker: ML
words: cornflakes, shredded wheat maybe. And all bran. We didn't have as many

speaker: ML
words: varieties then as we do now. And that always had to be on the table.

speaker: ML
words: There'd be a lot to put out. And then we'd have to fix the table so they

speaker: ML
